People/Ann-Copley.title: Ann Copley
People/Ann-Copley.content:
Ann Copley (1823–1909)

📊 View Family Tree for visual context.
Biographical Profile
Ann Copley is the matriarch of the West Virginia Copley line. Family narratives identify her as Ann Elizabeth Munday, born in 1823 in Kinawley, Ireland (County Fermanagh context), with immigration to the United States in childhood. However, because the primary marriage and birth records have not yet been located, her maiden surname remains a high-priority documentary verification gap.
She married Michael Copley (date/place unknown) and lived in Lewis County after the family’s 1843 land settlement. The 1850 census framework and family reconstruction identify her as mother of eight known children.
A persistent family tradition states that her father drowned in the Potomac River. This is plausible in context but currently unverified by contemporary records.
Key Place Links

Birthplace context: Kinawley, Ireland
Settlement context: Lewis County, West Virginia and Weston, West Virginia
Migration-corridor research hub: Baltimore, Maryland

Related Topic Pages

Murray Settlement — active research into whether “Munday” is a phonetic transcription of “Murray” (RQ-M5); Ann’s maiden name is central to the Murray anchor-family hypothesis
Irish Famine and Emigration
Irish Immigration to West Virginia
B&O Railroad Labor History

Family Relationships

Husband: Michael Copley
Children (G24):

Mary Copley Quinn
John Copley
Catherine “Kitty” Copley Hannon
Anne Copley
Bridget “Bitty” Copley Gillooly
Margaret Copley
Thomas “Tom” Copley
Sarah Copley



Known/Reported Life Data

Born: 1823, Kinawley parish, Fermanagh (reported in family narrative) — note: Ancestry.com tree gives Sep 1824; discrepancy unresolved
Died: 2 Nov 1909, Lewis County, West Virginia (Ancestry.com tree, unverified)
Burial: Weston, Lewis County, West Virginia (Ancestry.com tree, unverified)
Marriage: to Michael Copley, likely c.1838-1839 (inferred from first child birth year)

Research Gaps
Critical: RQ-M5 — Munday vs. Murray Hypothesis (April 23, 2026 Update)
Current Evidence Status:
graph LR
    QUEST["Was Ann Munday<br/>actually Ann Murray?"]
    
    FOR["✅ SUPPORTS MURRAY<br/>Hypothesis"]
    AGAINST["❌ SUPPORTS MUNDAY<br/>Hypothesis"]
    
    QUEST --> FOR
    QUEST --> AGAINST
    
    FOR --> E1["9+ Murrays in<br/>Griffith's 1862<br/>Kinawley"]
    FOR --> E2["'Munday'/'Murray'<br/>phonetically similar<br/>transcription error"]
    FOR --> E3["Murray family anchor<br/>would explain<br/>Michael's destination"]
    
    AGAINST --> E4["'Munday' appears in<br/>all family documents<br/>GEDCOM, Partlow"]
    AGAINST --> E5["Ann from Fermanagh<br/>not Roscommon<br/>where Murrays are"]
    
    E1 --> NEXT["NEXT: Search<br/>1823-1837<br/>Tithe Books"]
    E2 --> NEXT
    E3 --> NEXT
    E4 --> NEXT
    E5 --> NEXT
    
    NEXT --> TEST["VERDICT TEST:<br/>No Munday in Tithe?<br/>= Likely Murray"]
    
    style FOR fill:#c8e6c9
    style AGAINST fill:#ffccbc
    style NEXT fill:#fff9c4
    style TEST fill:#bbdefb

Status:
Status: Actively researched; significant evidence emerging
Tom Copley (April 2026) raised the possibility that “Munday” is a phonetic transcription of “Murray” — placing Ann inside the Murray family that named Murray’s Settlement. Phase 2 research (April 2026) yielded notable findings:
Evidence Against “Munday” as Independent Surname:

❌ No “Munday” entries in Griffith’s Valuation (1862) for Kinawley, County Fermanagh — exhaustively searched
✅ 9+ Murray entries documented in same Griffith’s Valuation (Bridget, Edward, James, John, Mary, Michael, Patrick, Peter)
❌ Kinawley Catholic parish records only begin December 11, 1835 — after Ann’s birth c. 1823-1824, making baptismal confirmation impossible
✅ Munday is a documented Irish surname (Anglicization of McAloon), but no Fermanagh/Kinawley records of it in the period

Next Research Steps (awaiting execution):

Tithe Applotment Books (1823–1837) — search at titheapplotmentbooks.nationalarchives.ie for “Munday” and “Murray” entries in Kinawley to test whether surname existed pre-emigration
Lewis County census (1840, 1850, 1860) — search for independent “Munday” family households; absence would strengthen Murray hypothesis
St. Michael’s Church records — contact Diocese of Wheeling-Charleston for marriage records 1838–1850 that might show Ann’s maiden name
Ship manifests — search Powhatan (Aug 20, 1838) and Kutusoff (1837) for female passengers with either surname

Preliminary verdict: If Tithe books and Lewis County records show no independent Munday family, and Griffith’s Valuation shows no Munday at Ann’s reported origin, the “Murray” hypothesis becomes the working explanation.
Other Research Gaps

Marriage record (Q3): No civil/church record yet found for Michael + Ann.
Immigration details: Port/date/manifest for Ann not yet resolved.
Father identity + drowning event (Q11/Q14): No coroner or newspaper confirmation yet found.
Wider Munday/Murray household reconstruction (Q14/Q15): Parents/siblings remain incomplete.

Acquisition Strategy

Search Catholic parish marriage registers in Potomac/B&O labor corridor communities (late 1830s to early 1840s).
Survey 1840-1850 census clusters near B&O settlements for Munday surname variants.
Use Chronicling America and regional newspaper repositories for Potomac drowning incidents matching family tradition.
Expand to PRONI and Irish/Fermanagh parish records for potential Ann Munday baptismal candidates.
Reconcile maiden-name evidence across gravestone, death certificates of children, and church sacramental records.

Source Citations

COPLEY HISTORY PART 1 final 2.pdf (sections on Ann’s origins, marriage uncertainty, Potomac tradition, and children).
/home/ubuntu/copley_research_analysis.md (Q3, Q11, Q14, Q15 framing).
/home/ubuntu/copley_research_findings.md (Ann profile and evidence reliability).
Chronicling America search portal: chroniclingamerica.loc.gov/
National Library of Ireland parish registers: registers.nli.ie/

People/Benjamin-Partlow.title: Benjamin Partlow
People/Benjamin-Partlow.content:
Benjamin Partlow (c. 1762–after 1834)
📊 View Family Tree for visual context.
Biographical Profile
Benjamin Partlow was an ancestor of Marion Elizabeth Partlow, the wife of Michael Joseph Copley. He is the earliest documented Partlow family member and a Revolutionary War veteran.

Born: c. 1762 (inferred: pension testimony July 1832 states he was “seventy years” old)
Residence at time of pension: Culpeper County, Virginia
Military service: Virginia militia, Revolutionary War
Pension commenced: 6 Apr 1834 (Rappahannock County pension roll, unverified)

Military Service
Benjamin Partlow served in the Virginia militia during the Revolutionary War. His July 18, 1832 pension statement before Justice of the Peace William Walden, Culpeper County, VA, documents:

Drafted in a company commanded by Capt. Coxen in Culpeper County, VA
Marched to the Albemarle Barracks as a guard for British prisoners quartered there; served approximately three months until discharged
Drafted again into a company commanded by Capt. Rogers; marched to Malvern Hills under Gen. Edward Stevens, where he was detailed from the line to drive a wagon
Drafted several other times for short intervals (total service: more than six months)
At time of petition (1832): confined to his house by paralysis; unable to travel to the county court

He declared he had no documentary proof of service but could prove his time at the Albemarle Barracks through living witness Thomas Thornhill Senior of Culpeper County.
His pension appeared on the Rappahannock County, Virginia pension roll (Statement &c.) listing him as a Private with annual allowance $20, pension commencing 6 Apr 1834, age ~74.
Connection to the Copley Family
Benjamin Partlow is in the Partlow ancestral line that eventually settled in Clark County, Illinois by the mid-1800s. The line descends through at least one generation (including a John Partlow, whose will named children Thomas Davenport, Lucy, Dorothy, Lewis, Polly, and Sarah) before reaching Nollie Franklin Partlow (b. 1874), Marion’s father. Marion’s middle name “Elizabeth” may honor the family line.
Family tradition holds the Partlows emigrated from Wales to Virginia before the Revolutionary War.
Research Gaps

Exact birth date and birthplace not established.
Wife’s name not confirmed.
Full chain from Benjamin → John Partlow → Nollie Franklin Partlow needs documentary reconstruction.
Pension file (NARA) may contain additional biographical detail.

Acquisition Strategy

NARA pension file search: Benjamin Partlow, Virginia militia, Revolutionary War (Culpeper County applicant)
Virginia state pension rolls and Culpeper County court records, 1830s
Clark County, Illinois probate and census records for the Partlow family, 1840–1880

Sources

Benjamin Partlow pension statement transcript, July 18, 1832, Culpeper County, VA (from Copley family research files; original source: National Archives pension records)
“Statement &c. of Rappahannock county, Virginia” pension roll — lists Benjamin Partlow as Private, pension commenced 6 Apr 1834
Marion Elizabeth Partlow — descendant connection context

People/Captain-John-Copley.title: Captain John Copley
People/Captain-John-Copley.content:
Captain John Copley (c. 1610–post 1657)
Biographical Profile

Birth: c. 1610, Worcestershire, England (exact date unknown)
Death: post-1657, Ireland (date and place unknown)
Status: Younger son of Thomas Copley Jr. of Bredon’s Norton, Worcestershire
Occupation: Military officer, entrepreneur, ironmaster
Wife: Margaret, daughter of George Newport of Rushock
Known residences: Worcestershire (1610–1634), Italy (1634), Kingswood Forest near Bristol (1656–57), Youghal, County Cork (1651), Ireland (post-1657)

Notable Life Events
1634 — In Italy: Documented in the 1634 Visitation of Worcestershire as being “in Italy.” Likely studying Italian iron-smelting techniques to support his later entrepreneurial ventures.
1642 — Parliamentary Petition: Petitioned the House of Commons concerning “some strange brave Exploits he would undertake in Ireland” during the Irish Rebellion of 1641. His petition was referred to the Lieutenant of the Tower and the Lieutenant of the Ordnance, but no record of acceptance survives. (Source: Journal of the House of Commons, Vol. 2)
1651 — Clerk of the Market, Youghal: Served as Clerk of the Market in Youghal, County Cork — historically significant as the Irish seat of Sir Walter Raleigh. His presence in Youghal is circumstantially consistent with family network ties to Raleigh’s circle. (Source: Richard Caulfield, ed., Council Book of the Corporation of Youghal)
1656–1657 — Kingswood Ironworks: Obtained a Cromwellian patent to produce iron using coal rather than charcoal. Established an iron-making venture at Kingswood Forest, near Bristol (~40 miles from Bredon’s Norton). Engaged Dud Dudley to improve the design of his bellows. The venture failed, and Copley subsequently moved to Ireland in 1657. (Source: Dud Dudley, Mettallum Martis, 1665, pp. 20–21)
Post-1657 — Ireland: Emigrated to Ireland; further records do not survive. A man with Cromwellian patent experience and engineering knowledge arriving in 1657 as the land settlement was being administered would have been well-positioned to receive a grant, but no specific record has yet been found.
Family Relationships
Parents: Thomas Copley Jr. (d. 1647) and his wife [[#|Margaret, daughter of Thomas Hanford of Wollashull]] (first marriage)
Siblings: Thomas (b. c. 1627), George, and others from Thomas Jr.’s marriages
Paternal grandfather: Thomas Copley Sr. (d. May 20, 1593, Bredon’s Norton)
Wife: Margaret, daughter of George Newport of Rushock — marriage date unknown; documented in the 1634 Visitation of Worcestershire (p. 26)
Children: Unknown; no primary sources confirm any children of Captain John
Research Gaps

Exact birth year (estimated c. 1610 from context)
Marriage date to Margaret Newport
Children’s names and fates
Specific date and place of death (post-1657 in Ireland)
Irish land grants or property records
Identity and fate of Margaret Newport after 1657

Sources

✅ Dud Dudley, Mettallum Martis: or, Iron Made with Pit-Coale and Sea-Coale (London, 1665), pp. 20–21. Available: Internet Archive.
✅ Journal of the House of Commons, Volume 2, 1640–1643 (London: HMSO, 1802). “Petition of John Copley.” Available: British History Online.
✅ Richard Caulfield, ed., Council Book of the Corporation of Youghal from 1610 to 1659, from 1666 to 1687 and from 1690 to 1800. “Captain Copley, Clerk of the Market, 1651.” Available: Google Books.
✅ P.W. King, “Dud Dudley’s Contribution to Metallurgy,” Historical Metallurgy (peer-reviewed journal) — confirms Copley reference in Dudley’s account.
✅ Visitations of Worcestershire, 1634, pp. 26–27. Names “Captain John Copley” and records him as “in Italy.” Available as searchable PDF online.
✅ Captain John Copley Research — comprehensive research page documenting the four independent primary sources across 1634–1657.

People/Catherine-Kitty-Copley-Hannon.title: Catherine Kitty Copley Hannon
People/Catherine-Kitty-Copley-Hannon.content:
Catherine “Kitty” Copley Hannon (b. 1848; d. 1878)
📊 View Family Tree for visual context.

People/Ellen-Bernadine-Nelle-Copley-Sard.title: Ellen Bernadine Nelle Copley Sardo
People/Ellen-Bernadine-Nelle-Copley-Sard.content:
Ellen Bernadine Nelle Copley Sardo (“Nelle”) (1897–1977)
📊 View Family Tree for visual context.
Biographical Profile
Ellen Bernadine “Nelle” Copley Sardo is a G24 daughter of John Copley and Mary Ellen Dolan Copley, and an anchor for the Sardo/Arena/Ruland branch.

Birth: Three conflicting sources — (1) family biography by grandchildren: Dec 23, 1897, Copley WV; (2) 1910 census age 13 implies 1896; (3) Ancestry.com tree: 25 Dec 1896, Sand Fork, Gilmer County WV. Discrepancy (Q45) remains open; primary birth record needed.
Named after: Her mother Ellen Dolan, and her aunt “Bitty” (Bernadine)
Education: DeSales Heights Academy (boarding school, Parkersburg WV); University of West Virginia (2 years); taught in a one-room schoolhouse; St. Joseph Hospital nursing school, Baltimore — RN 1926
Career: By age 35 (1932), Director of Nursing Education, St. Joseph Hospital, Baltimore; past president of the St. Joseph Nursing School Alumnae Association; director of volunteers (10 years); 1976: awarded 50-year service pin
Death: 11 Feb 1977
Parents: John Copley, Mary Ellen Dolan Copley
Career/occupation: Registered nurse and nursing educator (reported in family narrative synthesis)
Marriage: Robert Samuel Sardo (married 16 Aug 1931)

Family Relationships

Parents: John Copley, Mary Ellen Dolan Copley
Grandparents: Michael Copley, Ann Copley
Siblings:

Thomas E. Copley
Mary Copley Flesch
Anne Copley
Michael Joseph Copley


Spouse: Robert Samuel Sardo
Children (G25):

Sarah Ellen Sardo Arena
Mary Carmella Sardo Ruland



Research Gaps

Resolve birth-year discrepancy (1896 vs 1897) with primary records.
Document nursing education timeline and training institution(s).
Build fully sourced chronology of household moves and branch expansion.

Acquisition Strategy

Prioritize birth and marriage records; then census/city directory chain.
Search nursing school records, registration/licensure sources, and hospital archives where accessible.
Add obituary/church records for timeline closure and relationship confirmation.

Social Media & Online Presence

Not applicable from current high-confidence research set.

People/Fern-Loretta-Copley-MayBee.title: Fern Loretta Copley MayBee
People/Fern-Loretta-Copley-MayBee.content:
Fern Loretta Copley MayBee (1913–1997)
📊 View Family Tree for visual context. See also: Stephen Donald Copley, Mary Copely Giblin.
Biographical Profile

Birth: 26 December 1913, Manila, Crawford County, Iowa
Death: 27 August 1997 (age 83), Council Bluffs, Pottawattamie County, Iowa
Spouse: Edward Francis MayBee (b. 25 Feb 1900, d. 18 Apr 1982)
Generation: G27 (great-great-grandchild of Irish immigrant generation via Iowa Copley line)

Family Relationships
Parents:

Father: Stephen Donald Copley (birth/death dates unknown)
Mother: Effie Grace Coe

Spouse:

Edward Francis MayBee (b. 25 February 1900, d. 18 April 1982)
Married: date/location unknown
Edward’s occupation: Safety inspector (1939), First Aid Dept, WPA

Children:

Richard Charles MayBee (b. 12 January 1934, d. 13 September 2004)
Gerald E MayBee (b. 29 November 1935, d. August 1996)

Probable ancestor:
Fern is a great-great-grandchild of Mary Copely Giblin (b. 1814, Ireland; d. 1884, Iowa) through one of Mary’s children (likely Michael Giblin, b. 1845). Both Fern and Mary settled in Crawford County, Iowa, indicating a direct family lineage through the Iowa Copley branch.
Notable Life Events
Residences:

1939: 1806 Miami Street, Omaha, Douglas County, Nebraska (with husband Edward and son Richard)
1940 Census: 1810 Miami Street, Omaha, Douglas County, Nebraska
1950 Census: 2206 Bristol Street, Omaha, Douglas County, Nebraska
1997 (death): Council Bluffs, Pottawattamie County, Iowa

Employment:

1939 Census: Listed as wife of safety inspector
1950 Census: Stock Clerk, Furniture (working as head of household, divorced)

Family Status:

1940 Census: married to Edward MayBee
1950 Census: divorced, raising two sons as single mother

Research Gaps

Exact parentage of Stephen Donald Copley — Which of Mary Copely Giblin’s children was Fern’s grandfather? (John, Thomas, Martin, or Michael Giblin?)
Stephen Donald Copley biographical details — birth date, death date, mother’s maiden name (Coe?)
Edward Francis MayBee details — occupation history, birthplace confirmation, ancestry
Marriage record — Fern and Edward’s marriage date and location unknown
Children’s descendants — Did Richard or Gerald have children who continued the family line?
Burial location — Where is Fern buried? (not documented)

Sources

✅ MayBee Society genealogy database (maybeesociety.org), Individual Report for Fern Loretta Copley (I30855), accessed 22 April 2026. Sources cited in report: Ancestry.com Iowa Births and Christenings Index, Social Security Applications and Claims Index, 1940 and 1950 U.S. Census, Omaha City Directory 1939, Social Security Death Index.
✅ 1940 U.S. Census: Omaha, Douglas County, Nebraska, Roll T627-2269, Page 12A&B, ED 94-26. Lists Edward F. MayBee (head, M, W, 40, safety inspector), Fern L. MayBee (wife, F, W, 26), Richard Maybee (son, M, W, 6), Gerald Maybee (son, M, W, 4).
✅ 1950 U.S. Census: Omaha, Douglas County, Nebraska. Lists Fern L. Maybee (head, F, W, 36, divorced, stock clerk), Richard Maybee (son, M, W, 16), Jerry Maybee (son, M, W, 14).
⚠️ Inferred relationship to Mary Copely Giblin: Born in Crawford County, Iowa (same county where Mary settled and died 1884); father carries Copley surname; timeline suggests she is great-great-grandchild of Mary through one of her six children.

People/John-Copley.title: John Copley
People/John-Copley.content:
John Copley (1843–1925)
📊 View Family Tree for visual context.
Biographical Profile
John Copley was born 3 Nov 1843 near Weston, West Virginia (then Virginia), the son of Michael Copley and Ann Copley. He became the principal successor to the family farm established through the 1843 Hoffman agreement in the Copley Road area.
He married Mary Ellen Dolan Copley (date/place unresolved) and the couple had five children: Thomas E. Copley, Mary Copley Flesch, Anne Copley, Ellen Bernadine “Nelle” Copley Sardo, and Michael Joseph Copley.
John’s household sat at the center of the family’s economic transformation following the 1900 1900 Copley Oil Strike. Probate/lease details remain to be fully documented, but his generation is the key bridge between immigrant agrarian roots and later professional mobility of descendants.
Key Place Links

Lewis County, West Virginia
Weston, West Virginia
Cove Lick, West Virginia

Related Topic Pages

1900 Copley Oil Strike
Irish Immigration to West Virginia
Academic and Scientific Achievement

Family Relationships

Parents: Michael Copley, Ann Copley
Spouse: Mary Ellen Dolan Copley
Children (G24 in this branch convention):

Thomas E. Copley
Mary Copley Flesch
Anne Copley

Michael Joseph Copley


Grandchildren (G25 in Phase 1B scope):

Thomas Partlow Copley
Sarah Ellen Sardo Arena
Mary Carmella Sardo Ruland


Siblings:

Mary Copley Quinn
Catherine “Kitty” Copley Hannon
Anne Copley
Bridget “Bitty” Copley Gillooly
Margaret Copley
Thomas “Tom” Copley
Sarah Copley



Research Gaps

Marriage record with Mary Ellen (Q20) not located.
Civil War Quartermaster claim (Q28) unproven.
Late marriage/childbearing context (Q21) needs evidence-based explanation.
Oil lease and inheritance economics (Q24-Q26) not quantified.

Acquisition Strategy

Search civil/church marriage records in Lewis and Marshall county pathways.
File NARA military/pension request for all plausible John Copley candidates (including Quartermaster records, RG 92).
Extract land lease/probate records for 1899-1925 from Lewis County deed/probate books.
Build a dated household timeline from 1850-1920 census entries.

Source Citations

People/Judith-Ann-Todd-Copley.title: Judith Ann Todd Copley
People/Judith-Ann-Todd-Copley.content:
Judith Ann Todd Copley (“Judy”) (second wife of Stephen Michael Copley)
📊 View Family Tree for visual context.
Biographical Profile
Judith Ann Todd Copley (“Judy”) is the second wife of Stephen Michael Copley, whom she married in 1984. A distinguished materials scientist and academic administrator in her own right, she and Stephen are noted as the only husband and wife to both serve as Presidents of ASM International (ASMI), 30 years apart.

Origin: Wakefield, Yorkshire, England
Marriage: Stephen Michael Copley, 1984 (14 years his junior; they met when she joined the Materials Science Department at USC)
Current location: State College, Pennsylvania area
Occupation: Professor, Engineering Science and Mechanics Department, Penn State University (PSU)

Education

PhD — Cambridge University
Post-doctoral research: Imperial College of Science and Technology, London; State University of New York at Stonybrook; UC Berkeley

Career






























PeriodRoleInstitution~1984–1990Faculty, Materials ScienceUniversity of Southern California (USC)1990–1996Associate Professor; Associate Chair, Materials EngineeringIllinois Institute of Technology (IIT), Chicago2002–2022Department Head, Engineering Science and MechanicsPenn State University (PSU)2023–Full Professor (returned from sabbatical)Penn State University
Notable roles and honors:

President of the Society of Engineering Science (SES) — elected Fellow of SES
Vice President of Manufacturing, American Society of Mechanical Engineers (ASME) — elected Honorary Member of ASME
President, ASM International (ASMI) — making her and Stephen the only married couple to both serve as ASMI president
Member, Executive Committee of the Committee of Science and the Arts, Franklin Institute (Philadelphia)

Family Relationships

Spouse: Stephen Michael Copley (married 1984)
Child (G26): Amy E. Copley Geist (Amy Elizabeth, b. Jul 11, 1990, Hinsdale, IL)
Stepchildren (G26): Michael Copley (b. 1959), Sara Copley Cox, Philip Copley, Paul Copley, Peter Copley, Susan Copley, Stephen Joseph Copley

Residences

Palos Verdes Estates, CA (USC period)
Hinsdale, Illinois (IIT period; house with 100-year-old log cabin; French au pairs lived in log cabin)
State College, Pennsylvania (2002–present)

People/Marcia-Thornton-Copley.title: Marcia Thornton Copley
People/Marcia-Thornton-Copley.content:
Marcia Thornton Copley (first wife of Stephen Michael Copley)
📊 View Family Tree for visual context.
Biographical Profile
Marcia Thornton Copley was the first wife of Stephen Michael Copley, whom she married while both were students at UC Berkeley. She bore seven children and managed the household largely alone as Stephen pursued his research career. After the challenges of raising a severely disabled child strained the marriage, she and Stephen divorced in 1983. She later earned a college degree, launched a career in developmental disabilities services, and remarried.

First marriage: Stephen Michael Copley (married at UC Berkeley; divorced 1983)
Second marriage: Edward (Ed) Dawson — founder of Social Vocational Services (SVS), a non-profit serving the developmentally disabled
Education: BA in Human Services, Cal State Dominguez Hills (completed after divorce; education had been interrupted when she married Stephen)
Career: Client Service Coordinator, Harbor Regional Center — notably the first parent of a client to apply for and be employed by the Harbor Regional Center; dedicated her career to helping the developmentally disabled and their families

Family Relationships

First spouse: Stephen Michael Copley (divorced 1983)
Second spouse: Ed Dawson (founder of SVS)
Children with Stephen (G26):

Michael Copley (b. 1959)
Sara Copley Cox (b. Feb 3, 1961)
Philip Copley (b. 1962)
Paul Copley (b. 1964)
Peter Copley (b. Jan 12, 1966)
Susan Copley (b. 1967)
Stephen Joseph Copley (b. May 1, 1970; d. Jun 10, 2023)



Notes

Her son Stephen Joseph Copley was diagnosed as autistic and severely developmentally disabled; his care profoundly shaped the family and directly influenced Marcia’s career path.
SVS (Social Vocational Services), founded by her second husband Ed Dawson in 1973, grew to serve thousands of individuals at 80+ locations throughout California. Several of Stephen’s children also worked for SVS at various points.
Marcia is a descendant (through her mother’s side) of Patrick Breen, a survivor of the ill-fated Donner Party. Patrick established a ranch near Mission San Juan Bautista — where Stephen Joseph was buried in 2023.

Sources

~/Downloads/Part 1 Appendices .pdf — Stephen Michael Copley and Stephen Joseph Copley biographical sketches; Sara Cox biographical sketch.

People/Marion-Elizabeth-Partlow.title: Marion Elizabeth Partlow
People/Marion-Elizabeth-Partlow.content:
Marion Elizabeth Partlow (1905–1977)
📊 View Family Tree for visual context.
Biographical Profile
Marion Elizabeth Partlow was the wife of Michael Joseph Copley and mother of Stephen Michael Copley and Thomas Partlow Copley. Before marriage she had been an elementary school teacher. She was known as a devoted homemaker, excellent hostess (during Michael’s years as USDA lab director), and enthusiastic gardener who wrote a weekly wildflower column for a local California newspaper.

Birth: ~1905, Casey, Illinois (midway between Indianapolis and St. Louis)
Death: 26 Mar 1977, Berkeley, California (Ancestry.com tree, unverified)
Marriage: Michael Joseph Copley, 1933
Burial: Sunset View Cemetery, El Cerrito, California (with Michael) — confirmed by family; Ancestry.com tree incorrectly lists Oak Point, Clark County, IL
Occupation (pre-marriage): Elementary school teacher
Residence: Champaign IL → Wyndmoor PA → Berkeley / El Cerrito CA

Family of Origin
The Partlow family immigrated from Wales to Virginia before the Revolutionary War, then moved west to Indiana, then Illinois. They owned a large farm near Casey, Illinois. A Revolutionary War ancestor, Benjamin Partlow, served in the Virginia militia under Capt. Coxen and Capt. Rogers, guarding British prisoners at the Albemarle Barracks (Culpeper County, VA).

Father: Nollie Franklin Partlow (b. 8 Oct 1874, Johnson Township, Clark County, IL; d. 21 Nov 1956, Terre Haute, IN; buried Oak Point, Clark County, IL) — per Ancestry.com tree, unverified
Mother: Mary Alice Partlow (née unknown; b. 11 Oct 1880; d. 20 Jan 1962; buried Oak Point, Clark County, IL) — per Ancestry.com tree, unverified
Brothers: Harry Partlow, Raymond Partlow, Lester (“Leck”) Partlow
Sisters: Laura Partlow, Doris Partlow, Genevieve (“Gen”) Partlow

Family Relationships

Spouse: Michael Joseph Copley (married 1933; Michael died Sep 17 1988)
Children (G25):

Stephen Michael Copley (b. Apr 29, 1936, Champaign IL)
Thomas Partlow Copley (b. Oct 29, 1944, Philadelphia PA)



Notes

Thomas Partlow Copley’s middle name “Partlow” honors her maiden name.
After Michael’s retirement in 1968, Tom’s family moved back to Berkeley to help care for their parents after Marion’s death.

Sources

~/Downloads/Part 1 Appendices .pdf — Michael Joseph Copley, Stephen Copley, and Tom Copley biographical sketches.
Family Tree — internal branch mapping.

People/Mary-Copely-Giblin.title: Mary Copely Giblin
People/Mary-Copely-Giblin.content:
Mary Copely Giblin (1814–1884)
📊 View Family Tree for visual context. See also: Michael Copley Sr., Iowa Settlement.
Biographical Profile

Birth: 1814, Tully townland, Kilcorkey Civil Parish, County Roscommon, Ireland
Death: 27 October 1884 (age 69–70)
Death location: Westside, Crawford County, Iowa, United States
Burial: Saint Ann’s Cemetery, Vail, Crawford County, Iowa, United States
Spouse: John Giblin (married 1831, location unknown, Mary age 17)
Generation: G23 (Irish immigrant generation)

Family Relationships
Probable relationship to Michael Copley Sr.:
Mary Copely was born in 1814, the year after Michael Copley Sr. (b. ~1813). Both:

Share the “Copely” spelling (Catholic Roscommon branch variant)
Originated in the same parish cluster (Tully in Kilcorkey, near Kilgefin)
Emigrated to America in the same period (1830s–1840s)

Assessment: Mary is very likely Michael Sr.’s sister or a close relative from the Roscommon family network. The coincidence of birth year, spelling, location, and emigration timeline is too pronounced to be accidental. However, no primary source document has yet been located that confirms the exact relationship.
Siblings (potential):

Michael Copley Sr. (b. ~1813) — probable brother, emigrated to Lewis County, WV
[Other siblings unknown]

Spouse and Children:

Married: John Giblin in 1831 (location unknown) at age 17
Known children (son’s names and dates documented via WikiTree):

John Giblin (b. 1832, d. 1891)
Thomas Giblin (b. 1836, d. 1920)
Martin Giblin (b. 1843, d. 1907)
Michael Giblin (b. 1845, County Roscommon — baptized 26 Sep 1844 in Ballintubber and Ballymoe, Roscommon)
Mary Giblin (daughter; dates unknown)
Catharine Giblin (daughter; dates unknown)



Research note: The birth of Michael Giblin in County Roscommon in 1845 confirms that Mary and John remained in Ireland for several years after their 1831 marriage, emigrating to Iowa sometime after 1845. The name pattern (John, Thomas, Martin, Michael) in the children suggests strong Irish Catholic naming conventions and family continuity across the Atlantic.
Documented descendants (G27+):

Stephen Donald Copley (b. ~1880–1895) — likely son or grandson of one of Mary’s children (John, Thomas, Martin, Michael, Mary, or Catharine); carries Copley surname; married Effie Grace Coe

Merton Dale Copley (b. 30 May 1911, Crawford County, Iowa; d. 1978) — great-great-grandson of Mary
Fern Loretta Copley MayBee (b. 26 Dec 1913, Manila, Crawford County, Iowa; d. 27 Aug 1997) — great-great-granddaughter of Mary; documented through MayBee Society genealogy database

Spouse: Edward Francis MayBee (b. 1900, d. 1982)
Children: Richard Charles MayBee (b. 1934, d. 2004), Gerald E MayBee (b. 1935, d. 1996)





This documented line extends Mary Copely Giblin’s family tree into the 20th century and confirms the Iowa Copley branch’s persistence and growth through multiple generations.
Notable Life Events
Marriage (1831):
Mary married John Giblin in 1831 at age 17 (location of marriage ceremony unknown). The couple remained in Ireland for at least 14 years after marriage, during which they had at least six children, with the youngest child Michael born in County Roscommon in 1845.
Immigration to America:
Mary and her family emigrated from Ireland sometime after 1845 (specific year and port unknown) to the United States. They settled in Crawford County, Iowa, a region that attracted multiple Irish Catholic families during the mid-19th century. By the 1880 census, Mary was listed as age 60, born in Ireland, living in Hayes, Crawford County, Iowa.
Settlement in Iowa:
Mary’s presence in Crawford County, Iowa establishes a documented Copley/Copely branch in the American Midwest separate from the Michael Copley Sr. line in West Virginia. The emergence of the Iowa settlement suggests a broader family network dispersal pattern during Irish emigration.
Personal note (April 2026): Zach Copley, a G26 descendant of Michael Copley Sr., relocated to Iowa in 2025 with his family. His wife’s family is also from Iowa. The discovery of Mary Copely Giblin’s documented presence in Iowa (1814–1884) represents an unexpected family connection to his current residence — a potential ancestral thread linking Zach’s personal geography to his family’s 19th-century diaspora.
Research Gaps

Exact relationship to Michael Copley Sr. — Is she his sister, or a more distant relative? No primary source document has confirmed this.
Spouse’s full details — John Giblin (b. unknown, d. unknown); occupation and Irish origin unknown.
Children’s full names and marriages — We have names and birth years for six children, but need marriage records, occupations, and descendants to extend the family tree.
Exact emigration year — Mary emigrated sometime after 1845 (Michael Giblin born in Roscommon that year). Specific year, destination port, and ship name unknown.
Settlement pattern in Iowa — How did Mary and John end up in Crawford County? Did they follow family or community networks? Connection to other Irish Catholic families in the region?
Gravestone inscription — Does her stone at Saint Ann’s Cemetery provide spouse name, children names, or other biographical details?
Marriage record (1831) — Location of Mary and John’s 1831 marriage ceremony unknown; could be in Ireland or possibly in America if “1831” refers to something else.
Cemetery records — Mitchellville Cemetery — Records indicate Copley family stones in Mitchellville Cemetery (names: A. B., George B., Lavonne, Nellie V. Copley). May be descendants of Mary Copely Giblin or related to the Iowa Copley line; needs verification.

Sources

✅ Find a Grave Memorial #8468680 — Mary Copely Giblin (1814–1884), Saint Ann’s Cemetery, Vail, Crawford County, Iowa. Accessed April 22, 2026.
✅ WikiTree, profile Copeley-3 — Mary (Copeley) Giblin. Provides marriage date (1831), spouse name (John Giblin), children’s names and birth/death years (John 1832–1891, Thomas 1836–1920, Martin 1843–1907, Michael 1845–1925), daughters Mary and Catharine, 1880 census data, and baptism record. Accessed April 22, 2026. www.wikitree.com/wiki/Copeley-3
✅ Baptism record (primary source via WikiTree): “Maria Coply’s child Michaelm Giblin baptism on 26 Sep 1844 in Ballintubber and Ballymoe, Roscommon, Ireland” — confirms Mary’s presence in Roscommon parish in 1844 and documents her son Michael’s baptism.
✅ 1880 Census record (via WikiTree): Mary Giblin, age 60, born in Ireland, Hayes, Crawford County, Iowa — documents her residence in Iowa by the 1880 census.
⚠️ John Grenham Irish Placename Database — Tully townland, Kilcorkey Civil Parish, County Roscommon. Confirms Tully is ~10 km from Termon Beg (Kilkeevin), in the broader Castlerea/Kilgefin region where other Copleys are documented.
⚠️ Inferred relationship: Born 1814 (year after Michael Sr., b. ~1813); same “Copely” spelling (Catholic variant); same Roscommon parish cluster origin; same emigration era (1830s–1840s). Tom Copley, April 2026.

Acquisition Strategy

Irish civil registration: Search for Mary Copely’s birth/baptism record in Kilcorkey parish registers (1813–1815) to confirm parentage and relationship to Michael Sr.
Iowa settlement records: Search Crawford County historical records, land deeds, and church records (Saint Ann’s Parish) for Giblin family and Copley/Copely references.
Gravestone documentation: Obtain full transcription of Mary’s gravestone at Saint Ann’s Cemetery, Vail, Iowa, which may list maiden name, spouse, children, or other family information.
Ship manifests: Search passenger records for Irish emigration to Iowa (1830s–1840s) under “Copely,” “Giblin,” or related names.

People/Michael-Copley-Sr.title: Michael Copley Sr.
People/Michael-Copley-Sr.content:
Michael Copley Sr. (1813–1897)
📊 View Family Tree for visual context.
Disambiguation

Not to be confused with Michael Joseph Copley (1898–1988), his grandson.
Not to be confused with Michael Copley (b. 1959), his great-great-grandson in the Stephen branch.

Biographical Profile
Michael Copley is the documented immigrant patriarch of the Copley family branch that settled in Lewis County, West Virginia. Family history and grave-marker evidence identify him as born 29 Sep 1813 in the parish of Kilgefin, County Roscommon, Ireland, and deceased in Lewis County, West Virginia in January 1897 (gravestone: 19 Jan 1897; narrative text includes 18 Jan 1897, a minor discrepancy to track).
He emigrated in the late 1830s and is strongly associated with the passenger record of Michael “Copely” arriving in New York on the Powhatan in August 1838. A possible precursor record — the brig Kutusoff manifested in 1837 — may reflect an earlier sibling arrival.


Family narrative and contextual evidence indicate an early labor phase connected to the Baltimore & Ohio Railroad (B&O) corridor, especially around Baltimore, Maryland and Potomac-route communities, followed by transition to settled agriculture.
In 1843, Michael and his brother Patrick Copley entered a purchase agreement for a 200-acre tract from Weeden Hoffman in what became the Cove Lick area near Weston. This transaction marks the family’s permanent establishment in the region.



Family Relationships

Spouse: Ann Copley (family tradition/secondary narratives identify her as Ann Elizabeth Munday)
Children (G24):

Mary Copley Quinn
John Copley
Catherine “Kitty” Copley Hannon
Anne Copley
Bridget “Bitty” Copley Gillooly
Margaret Copley
Thomas “Tom” Copley
Sarah Copley


Siblings (Ireland/U.S. migration context): Patrick Copley, Bridget Copley Reynolds, Catherine Kitty Copley Hannon, William Copley

Timeline Anchors

1813-09-29: Born, Kilgefin parish, Roscommon (per gravestone tradition).
1838-08 (manifest date 1838-08-20): Probable New York arrival on Powhatan as “Michael Copely.”
1838-1843 (inferred): Probable B&O labor period along Potomac corridor.
1843: 200-acre agreement with Weeden Hoffman, Lewis County.
1897-01: Death in Lewis County, WV.

Related Topic Pages

Irish Famine and Emigration
B&O Railroad Labor History
Irish Immigration to West Virginia

Research Gaps

Parents in Kilgefin (Q1): No direct parish record chain yet linking Michael to named parents.
Marriage record (Q3): Date/place for marriage to Ann remains unlocated.
Naturalization (Q19): Date, court, and certificate details unknown.
B&O service detail (Q16/Q17): Specific payroll/crew records not yet located.
Death-date discrepancy: 18 Jan vs 19 Jan 1897 across derivative narratives.

Acquisition Strategy

Irish parish registers (NLI): Systematic baptism search c.1808-1820 in Kilgefin and nearby Roscommon parishes for Michael + siblings.
Naturalization search by corridor: County/state courts in MD/VA/WV aligned to B&O routes; extract occupation and arrival data.
Marriage targeting: Catholic sacramental records and civil records in Potomac-adjacent communities (Harpers Ferry–Cumberland corridor).
Railroad records: Investigate B&O archival holdings and local historical collections for labor/contractor references.
Death reconciliation: Compare grave transcription, cemetery registers, and any obituary/church burial record.

Source Citations

COPLEY HISTORY PART 1 final 2.pdf (uploaded source; pp. 3-17 for immigrant narrative, gravestone text, 1843 land agreement context).
copley_research_analysis (/home/ubuntu/copley_research_analysis.md) — synthesis of Q1, Q3, Q19, migration and settlement findings.
copley_research_findings (/home/ubuntu/copley_research_findings.md) — reliability and research strategy framework.
Powhatan manifest reference via findings synthesis (NARA microfilm pathway).
B&O historical context: www.wvencyclopedia.org/entries/830

People/Patrick-Copley.title: Patrick Copley
People/Patrick-Copley.content:
Patrick Copley (c.1818–unknown)
📊 View Family Tree for visual context.
Biographical Profile
Patrick Copley is the brother of Michael Copley, the immigrant patriarch of the West Virginia Copley line. Patrick emigrated from Ireland alongside Michael and co-established the family’s first land holding in Lewis County, West Virginia.

Birth: c.1818, Kilgefin, County Roscommon, Ireland (inferred from age on 1838 passenger manifest)
Death: unknown
Emigration: August 20, 1838 — arrived New York aboard the Powhatan, listed as “Pat Copely,” age 20, alongside “Michael Copely,” age 22

Key Events

1838: Arrived New York on the Powhatan with brother Michael.
1843: Co-signed the Weeden Hoffman land deed with Michael — a 200-acre purchase in what became the Cove Lick area near Weston, WV. This deed established the family’s permanent settlement in Lewis County.
Post-1843: Patrick’s subsequent life is undocumented. It is unknown whether he remained in Lewis County, moved elsewhere, or died young.

Family Relationships

Sibling: Michael Copley (immigrant patriarch)
Possible siblings: Bridget Copley Reynolds, Catherine “Kitty” Copley Hannon, William Copley

Research Gaps

Parents’ names and parish records in Kilgefin not yet confirmed.
No records located for Patrick after the 1843 deed — fate unknown.
Whether Patrick married or had descendants is unconfirmed.
The 1837 Kutusoff manifest may record an earlier sibling emigration — relationship to Patrick unclear.

Acquisition Strategy

Search Lewis County deed and probate records after 1843 for Patrick Copley.
Check 1850 US Federal Census for Patrick Copley in Lewis County and surrounding WV counties.
Survey Catholic parish marriage registers in the B&O corridor (Baltimore–Cumberland–Weston) for a Patrick Copley.

Sources

~/Downloads/COPLEY HISTORY PART 1 final 2.pdf — 1843 deed context and sibling mention.
Ship Powhatan passenger manifest, August 20, 1838 — “Pat Copely” (age 20) traveling with “Michael Copely” (age 22).
Family Tree — internal branch mapping.

People/Robert-Cox.title: Robert Cox
People/Robert-Cox.content:
Robert “Bobby” Anthony Cox (b. October 3, 1988)

📊 View Family Tree for visual context.
Biographical Profile
Robert “Bobby” Anthony Cox is a G27 descendant in the Stephen Michael Copley line — the son of Sara Copley Cox and Robert (Bob) Austin Cox.

Full name: Robert Anthony Cox
Birth: October 3, 1988, Sacramento, California
Parents: Sara Copley Cox and Robert (Bob) Austin Cox
Current location: Granite Bay, California (lives in his mother’s house; Sara is now itinerant for work)
Occupation: Owner, landscaping business
Partner: Nicole Brune (dating since July 2022)

Biography
Bobby was born in Sacramento, CA shortly after his parents moved from Southern California to the Sacramento area. His parents divorced in 1996 when he was seven, and he split time between his mother in Granite Bay and his father (and stepmother Diana) in Rancho Cordova. His early years were marked by school changes and social difficulty, but he found his footing in high school through friendships and weightlifting.
After high school he worked various jobs (Ace Hardware, Cost Plus World Market, Farmer’s Rice Co., landscaping) before dedicating himself to building his own landscaping business, which he pursued alongside academic coursework in horticulture and other subjects. He is described in the family history as deeply grateful to his family and committed to a mindful, purposeful lifestyle.

Father: Robert Austin Cox (hails from Torrance, CA; advertising career)
Stepmother: Diana (originally from Brea, CA; married Bob 1998; died October 2019)

Family Relationships

Parents: Sara Copley Cox, Robert Austin Cox
Maternal grandparents: Stephen Michael Copley, Marcia Thornton Copley
Maternal great-grandparents: Michael Joseph Copley, Marion Elizabeth Partlow

People/Stephen-Donald-Copley.title: Stephen Donald Copley
People/Stephen-Donald-Copley.content:
Stephen Donald Copley
📊 View Family Tree for visual context. See also: Fern Loretta Copley MayBee, Mary Copely Giblin.
Biographical Profile

Birth: date unknown; estimated 1880–1895 based on having a daughter born 1913
Death: date unknown
Generation: Likely G25–G26 (son or grandson of Mary Copely Giblin’s children)

Family Relationships
Children:

Merton Dale Copley (b. 30 May 1911, Crawford County, Iowa; d. 1978)
Fern Loretta Copley MayBee (b. 26 Dec 1913, Manila, Crawford County, Iowa; d. 27 Aug 1997, Council Bluffs, Iowa)

Spouse: Edward Francis MayBee (b. 1900, d. 1982)
Children: Richard Charles MayBee (b. 1934, d. 2004), Gerald E MayBee (b. 1935, d. 1996)



Mother (unknown):

Likely surname: Coe (Fern’s mother was Effie Grace Coe; family name pattern suggests mother may have been named Coe)

Probable ancestor:
Stephen Donald Copley is very likely a son or grandson of Mary Copely Giblin (b. 1814 Ireland, d. 1884 Iowa) through one of her six children: John Giblin (1832–1891), Thomas Giblin (1836–1920), Martin Giblin (1843–1907), Michael Giblin (1845–1925), or daughters Mary or Catharine. The Copley surname (rather than Giblin) suggests he either took his mother’s maiden name or was born to a Giblin father who used the Copley family name.
Notable Life Events
Geographic location:
Stephen Donald Copley’s daughter Fern was born in Manila, Crawford County, Iowa — the same county where Mary Copely Giblin settled and died in 1884. This geographic continuity strongly supports a direct family lineage through the Iowa Copley branch.
Research Gaps

Full biographical details — birth date, death date, burial location all unknown
Parentage — Which of Mary Copely Giblin’s children was Stephen Donald’s parent? (Key research priority)
Mother’s identity — Relationship to Effie Grace Coe unknown; was she Stephen’s wife or mother?
Occupation and residence history — no documented records yet
Marriage record — spouse and marriage date unknown
Merton’s details — Death location, burial location, spouse, and children all unknown (1978 death documented but details sparse)

Acquisition Strategy

Crawford County, Iowa records: Search land deeds, marriage records, census records (1900–1930) for “Stephen Donald Copley” or “Stephen Copley”
Giblin family records: Cross-reference with descendants of Mary Copely Giblin’s children (John, Thomas, Martin, Michael, Mary, Catharine) to identify which line produced Stephen Donald
Church records: Search St. Ann’s Parish (Vail, Iowa) or other Crawford County Catholic parishes for Copley/Giblin family sacramental records
Fern’s marriage record: Marriage of Fern Loretta Copley to Edward Francis MayBee may list her father’s full name, occupation, and dates

Sources

✅ MayBee Society genealogy database (maybeesociety.org), Individual Report for Fern Loretta Copley (I30855), lists Stephen Donald Copley as father of Fern Loretta Copley, accessed 22 April 2026.
⚠️ Inferred relationship to Mary Copely Giblin: Geographic location (Crawford County, Iowa; same as Mary’s settlement), surname pattern (Copley retained rather than Giblin), and timeline (Fern born 1913, 29 years after Mary’s death 1884, suggesting Stephen was born ~1880s–1890s to one of Mary’s children who would have been born 1832–1925) all support a direct family lineage.

Related Pages

Mary Copely Giblin — probable great-grandmother or great-great-grandmother
Fern Loretta Copley MayBee — daughter
Murray Settlement — ancestral settlement in Lewis County, WV
Iowa Settlement — family diaspora destination

People/Stephen-Joseph-Copley.title: Stephen Joseph Copley
People/Stephen-Joseph-Copley.content:
Stephen Joseph Copley (1970–2023)
📊 View Family Tree for visual context.
Biographical Profile
Stephen Joseph Copley was born May 1, 1970 at Yale-New Haven Hospital, New Haven, Connecticut — the seventh and youngest child of Stephen Michael Copley and Marcia Thornton Copley. Shortly after his birth, the family relocated to Southern California when his father accepted a position at USC.
He was diagnosed early in life as autistic and severely developmentally disabled. As he grew older he required constant attention, was nonverbal, and prone to wandering. His care profoundly shaped the entire Copley family and directly influenced the careers and life paths of several of his siblings.

Birth: May 1, 1970, Yale-New Haven Hospital, New Haven, Connecticut
Death: June 10, 2023 (in his sleep, age 53)
Burial: San Juan Bautista, CA, August 8, 2023
Residence: Placed as a client of Harbor Regional Center; cared for at the Intercommunity Exceptional Children’s Home, Long Beach, CA; returned home on weekends
Legal status: Became a full-time ward of the State of California (at age 13, when parents divorced in 1983)

His father, Stephen Michael Copley, wrote his biographical sketch in the family history. A Celebration of Life ceremony was held September 7, 2023, at Skylinks Golf Course, Long Beach, CA.

“Stephen taught us all a new meaning of love. Through Stephen, we met other young children, individuals with disabilities and their parents, who otherwise we never would have known.” — Stephen Michael Copley

Family Relationships

Parents: Stephen Michael Copley, Marcia Thornton Copley
Siblings (G26): Michael Copley (b. 1959), Sara Copley Cox, Philip Copley, Paul Copley, Peter Copley, Susan Copley
Half-sister: Amy E. Copley Geist
Spouse: none; children: none

Sources

People/Thomas-Copley-Jr.title: Thomas Copley Jr.
People/Thomas-Copley-Jr.content:
Thomas Copley Jr. (c. 1580–1647)
Biographical Profile

Birth: c. 1580 (estimated)
Death: 1647, Bredon’s Norton, Worcestershire, England
Status: Eldest son of Thomas Copley Sr.; lord of Bredon’s Norton manor
Seat: Bredon’s Norton, Worcestershire
Documented: Present at the 1634 Visitation of Worcestershire (listed as #1 son)

Biographical Summary
Thomas Copley Jr. inherited Bredon’s Norton manor from his father and held the estate through the English Civil War era. He was present at the 1634 Visitation of Worcestershire, where his family is documented in detail. Following his death in 1647, the manor was sold in 1649, ending the family’s direct presence in the village and leaving his younger sons without inheritance — a circumstance that directly contributed to his brother Captain John Copley’s emigration to Ireland in 1657.
Family Relationships
Father: Thomas Copley Sr. (d. May 20, 1593)
Siblings: Captain John Copley, William of London, Nicholas of London, Arthur Copley, and sisters Elizabeth, Mary, Susan, Margaret, Katherine, Jane, Hester, Anne, and Bridget
First Wife: Margaret, daughter of Thomas Hanford of Wollashull — marriage details unknown
Second Wife: Frances, daughter of Sir George Browne (2nd son of Viscount Montagu)
Children:

Thomas (b. c. 1627, aged approximately 7 in 1634) — grandson of Thomas Sr.
George — brother of Thomas

Notable Events
1634 — Visitation of Worcestershire: Present at the 1634 Visitation of Worcestershire (listed as #1 eldest son), confirming his status as lord of Bredon’s Norton at that date.
1647 — Death: Died in 1647, at the height of the English Civil War, leaving the estate in uncertain hands during political upheaval.
1649 — Manor Sale: The Bredon’s Norton manor was sold two years after his death, ending the family’s 16th–17th-century seat in Worcestershire.
Research Gaps

Exact birth and death dates
Details of both marriages (dates, circumstances)
Specific property holdings and estate administration
Civil War participation or allegiances (if any)
Complete list of children and their fates
Religious details beyond family Catholic recusancy
Relationship to the Hanford and Browne/Montagu families

Sources

✅ Visitations of Worcestershire, 1634, pp. 26–27. Pedigree chart documenting Thomas Jr., his wives, and children. Available as searchable PDF online.
✅ bredonsnorton.co.uk — village history documenting Thomas Jr.’s death in 1647 and the manor’s sale in 1649.
✅ Captain John Copley Research — research page documenting the family context and the broader Bredon’s Norton Copley line.

People/Thomas-Copley-Sr.title: Thomas Copley Sr.
People/Thomas-Copley-Sr.content:
Thomas Copley Sr. (c. 1540–1593)
Biographical Profile

Birth: c. 1540–1550 (estimated)
Death: May 20, 1593, Bredon’s Norton, Worcestershire, England
Estate administration: Worcester diocese, 1593
Status: Patriarch of the Copley family of Bredon’s Norton; son of [[#|William Copley]] (who married Bennett Chaloner of Woolbedding, Sussex)
Seat: Bredon’s Norton, Vale of Evesham, Worcestershire
Known associates: Sir Walter Raleigh (family tradition)

Biographical Summary
Thomas Copley Sr. was the founding figure of the Worcestershire Copley line. He held the manor of Bredon’s Norton in the Vale of Evesham, Worcestershire, and is documented in the 1634 Visitation of Worcestershire as the patriarch of a large, well-connected family of the English gentry. An inscription in Bredon Church commemorates his death on May 20, 1593.
Local tradition associates Thomas with Sir Walter Raleigh, crediting the family with introducing tobacco and potatoes to the Vale of Evesham — occupations consistent with Raleigh’s circle. The family held Catholic recusant sympathies, which may have influenced their descendants’ decisions and movements in Ireland.
Family Relationships

Crest: A covered cup or

(Source: Visitations of Worcestershire, 1634, p. 26)

People/Weeden-Hoffman.title: Weeden Hoffman
People/Weeden-Hoffman.content:
Weeden Hoffman
Biographical Profile
Weeden Hoffman was a Lewis County, Virginia landowner and the counterparty in the pivotal January 5, 1843 land deed that established the Copley family’s permanent settlement in what became West Virginia. On that date, Hoffman sold a 200-acre tract in the Cove Lick/Copley Road area near Weston to Michael Copley and his brother Patrick Copley.
This transaction is the single most important documentary anchor in the Copley family’s American history, marking the transition from immigrant laborers on the B&O Railroad corridor to settled landowners in Lewis County.
Historical Significance

The deed is dated January 5, 1843 and recorded in Lewis County deed books.
The tract became known as the Copley farm and remained in the family for generations.
The area around it became known as Copley Road, a place name that persists to this day.

Research Gaps

Weeden Hoffman’s full biography, family, and other land transactions in Lewis County are not yet compiled.
The deed itself has not been directly transcribed into this vault — a copy from Lewis County deed books would confirm acreage, price, and legal description.

Sources

~/Downloads/COPLEY HISTORY PART 1 final 2.pdf — land deed context and Copley settlement narrative.
Family Tree — internal context.
Lewis County, WV deed records (Weston courthouse) — primary source for the deed itself.

People/William-Copley.title: William Copley
People/William-Copley.content:
William Copley (c.1820s–unknown)
📊 View Family Tree for visual context.
Biographical Profile
William Copley is believed to be a sibling of Michael Copley, the Irish immigrant patriarch who settled in Lewis County, West Virginia. Family tradition holds that William emigrated to Australia rather than to the United States, making him the only known member of the Kilgefin Copley siblings to take a different emigration path. No documentary record has yet been located to confirm this tradition.

Birth: c.1820s, Kilgefin, County Roscommon, Ireland (presumed; based on sibling generation context)
Death: unknown
Emigration destination (family tradition): Australia — route, port, and date unconfirmed

Family Relationships

Sibling: Michael Copley (immigrant patriarch, Lewis County WV)
Possible siblings: Patrick Copley, Bridget Copley Reynolds, Catherine “Kitty” Copley Hannon

Research Gaps (Q6)

No passenger manifest or Australian immigration record has been located for a William Copley matching this profile.
No death, burial, or descendant records in Australia have been confirmed.
It is unknown whether William emigrated alone or with family members.
The family tradition of Australian emigration may derive from oral history; the original source is not documented.

Acquisition Strategy

Search Australian convict and free settler records (1820s–1860s) for William Copley or Copely variants.
Check National Archives of Australia and state archives (NSW, Victoria) for Irish immigrant arrivals from Roscommon.
Search New South Wales and Victorian electoral rolls and census substitutes (1850s–1880s) for Copley surname.
Cross-reference with Irish passenger lists departing via Liverpool or Cork for Australian-bound ships.

Sources

~/Downloads/COPLEY HISTORY PART 1 final 2.pdf — family tradition of Australian emigration; listed as sibling of Michael Copley Sr.
Family Tree — internal branch mapping.
Phase 1 Questions and Answers.md — Q6: William Copley’s Australian fate (unresolved).

Phase-1-Questions-and-Answers.title: Phase 1 Questions and Answers
Phase-1-Questions-and-Answers.content:
Phase 1 Questions and Answers
This page addresses all Phase 1 quick-win questions from the strategy analysis and documents what is known, unknown, and the next action.
📊 Use Family Tree as the visual reference when mapping each research question to specific people and branches.
Q23 — Who was the “Tom” at Chancellorsville?
Current answer
Family tradition references a “Tom” who fought at Chancellorsville, but this appears to be an identity mismatch in the current compiled narrative. No reliable documentary chain yet links the story to a clearly identified person in this line (including Thomas E. Copley).
Gap analysis

Known: A family memory exists; narrative inconsistency has been flagged.
Unknown: Which Thomas (if any) served; exact relationship to core line.

Strategy to close gap

Build collateral-line matrix of all Thomas/Tom Copleys born c.1820–1850.
Search Civil War CMSR/pension indexes and unit rosters.
Reconcile with family tree timelines to eliminate impossible candidates.

Citations

Internal analysis note (Q23): file-based synthesis from family records.
NARA military records gateway: www.archives.gov/research/military/civil-war


Q45 — What is Nelle’s correct birth year (1896 vs 1897)?
Current answer
Working conclusion is 1897 (often listed as Dec 23, 1897), but conflicting references in family materials require direct primary verification.
Gap analysis

Known: Both 1896 and 1897 appear in compiled family text.
Unknown: Definitive civil/church record date.

Strategy to close gap

Retrieve Lewis County birth register entry and/or baptismal register.
Cross-check with earliest census age reporting and Social Security-era documents.

Citations

FamilySearch profile context for descendants: ancestors.familysearch.org/en/MVDR-37G/michael-joseph-copley-1898-1988
Lewis County genealogy research guide: www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Lewis_County,_West_Virginia_Genealogy


Q28 — Did John Copley serve in the Union/Quartermaster system?
Current answer
No conclusive proof yet. Existing web hints are weak and do not meet proof standards.
Gap analysis

Known: Family claim exists.
Unknown: Service unit, service dates, rank, pension trail.

Strategy to close gap

Request Compiled Military Service Records and pension records from NARA.
Specifically target Quartermaster Department records (Record Group 92).
Verify identity through county, age, and dependent records.

Citations

NARA Civil War records: www.archives.gov/research/military/civil-war
NARA military service requests: www.archives.gov/veterans/military-service-records


Q20 — Marriage date/place for John Copley and Mary Ellen Dolan Copley
Current answer
Marriage is inferred from child chronology and family records, but no definitive marriage certificate/parish entry has been located.
Gap analysis

Known: Couple identity and descendants are well-supported.
Unknown: Exact ceremony date, parish/county, officiant/witnesses.

Strategy to close gap

Search Lewis and Marshall County marriage books (c. 1880–1890).
Search Catholic sacramental registers in likely diocesan repositories.
Check newspaper marriage notices for both surnames.

Citations

Lewis County genealogy guide: www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Lewis_County,_West_Virginia_Genealogy


Q22 — Reconstruct the Dolan Family (Mary Ellen’s siblings)
Current answer
Parents are identified as Michael and Elizabeth Dolan in compiled materials, but sibling-level documentation remains sparse.
Gap analysis

Known: Core parental names and Moundsville connection are repeatedly cited.
Unknown: Full sibling list, migration pattern, occupations, parish ties.

Strategy to close gap

Build household reconstructions from census decades 1860–1910.
Search Marshall County probate/deed/church records for Dolan witnesses and heirs.
Use cluster research (neighbors/sponsors/godparents) to separate same-name individuals.

Citations

WV state and county archival leads via FamilySearch wiki: www.familysearch.org/en/wiki/Lewis_County,_West_Virginia_Genealogy
Dolan surname resource (secondary): www.wikitree.com/genealogy/DOLAN


Q19 — Naturalization date/place for Michael Copley
Current answer
No naturalization document has been found yet; likely jurisdictions include early B&O corridor counties in MD/VA/WV.
Gap analysis

Known: Immigration period c. 1837–1838 is strongly supported by manifests.
Unknown: Court, petition date, witnesses, declaration/final papers.

Strategy to close gap

Search county-level naturalization indexes along rail corridor.
Correlate census citizenship columns with county court records.
Review NARA regional holdings and state archives.

Citations

NARA immigration/naturalization research: www.archives.gov/research/immigration
Passenger-list context (Powhatan/Kutusoff synthesis in findings report).


Q6 — What happened to William Copley in Australia?
Current answer
A plausible family hypothesis links William to anti-tithe unrest and transportation, but there is currently no direct documentary confirmation.
Gap analysis

Known: Story aligns with historical environment of the Irish Tithe War.
Unknown: Court file, ship transport record, colonial assignment, later life.

Strategy to close gap

Search Australian convict indexes and transport registers for surname variants.
Search Irish assize/petty sessions records c.1830–1840.
Link by age/place/family associates to avoid false positives.

Citations

Trove (National Library of Australia): trove.nla.gov.au
Convict Records (Australia): convictrecords.com.au


Q33–Q35 — Missing living-family biographical sketches
Current answer
Biographies for some modern descendants are uneven in depth. Core lines are known, but editorial completeness varies.
Gap analysis

Known: Major branches and several professional trajectories are documented.
Unknown: Standardized biographical summaries, source-backed timelines, and oral-history annotations for each living branch.

Strategy to close gap

Use a structured interview template for willing family members.
Collect publication/professional profile links and archive with permissions.
Tag oral-history claims separately from record-backed claims.

Citations

Method guidance from compiled analysis and findings documents.


Linked Research Entities
People and lines: Michael Copley, Ann Elizabeth Munday Copley, John Copley, Mary Ellen Dolan Copley, Ellen Bernadine “Nelle” Copley Sardo, Michael Joseph Copley, Dolan Family
Places and topics: Kilgefin, Roscommon, Lewis County, West Virginia, B&O Railroad Labor, Copley No. 1 Oil Strike
See also: Family Tree, People Directory, The Copley Family Narrative

Places/Baltimore-Maryland.title: Baltimore Maryland
Places/Baltimore-Maryland.content:
Baltimore, Maryland
Geographic Information

🗺️ Google Maps: View on Google Maps
Coordinates: 39.2904, -76.6122
Current administration: Independent City of Baltimore, Maryland, USA.
Historical names/boundaries: “Baltimore City” and Archdiocese-based parish references are common in 19th-century records.

Historical Context (1800s-1900s)
Baltimore was a major immigrant port and the strategic hub of the B&O Railroad system. Irish migrants moving into rail labor and western settlement corridors commonly passed through or worked from Baltimore-linked networks.

Economy: port commerce, rail administration, manufacturing.
Immigration pattern: strong Irish migration and labor recruitment base.
Record significance: rich Catholic and civil archival systems.

Copley Family Connection

Key context point for B&O Railroad Labor History and inferred early labor phase of Michael Copley Sr.
Important migration node for Irish Famine and Emigration.
High-priority location for locating potential marriage records for Michael Copley Sr and Ann Copley.
Also part of broader Maryland branch context for descendants like Ellen Bernadine Nelle Copley Sardo.

Related Topic Pages

B&O Railroad Labor History
Irish Famine and Emigration
Irish Immigration to West Virginia

Research Resources

Archdiocese of Baltimore Archives (parish marriages/baptisms).
Maryland State Archives (civil and county/city records).
B&O Railroad Museum Library & Archives.
Historic newspapers (Chronicling America, Newspapers.com, GenealogyBank).

Acquisition Strategy

Search 1838-1843 Catholic marriages for Copley/Munday variants.
Query B&O worker/contractor references in archival indexes.
Build Baltimore-to-Cumberland corridor chronology tied to family events.

Current administration: County Roscommon, Connacht, Republic of Ireland.
Historical names/boundaries: records distributed across parish, barony, and estate systems.

Historical Context (1800s-1900s)
Roscommon was a core agrarian county with high tenancy pressure and persistent emigration. For genealogy, county-level context is essential because families could appear across neighboring parishes and spellings.

Economy: agriculture/tenancy with limited industrial absorption.
Migration pattern: sustained movement to U.S. labor and settlement fronts.
Research implication: triangulate church, land, tax, and estate records.

Copley Family Connection

Regional context for the pre-emigration generation tied to Michael Copley Sr.
Supports parish-level work in Kilgefin.
Helps frame unresolved parentage and sibling reconstruction questions.

Current administration: Rural locality near Weston, Lewis County, West Virginia.
Historical names/boundaries: appears in road/tract descriptions more than as a formal town name.

Historical Context (1800s-1900s)
Cove Lick represents land-based settlement geography: roads, tract lines, and deed descriptions rather than municipal addresses. For immigrant farm families, this was the practical center of household continuity.

Economy: farm production with later proximity to oil-era activity.
Record style: deeds, plats, metes-and-bounds descriptions.
Significance: transition from wage labor to rooted land tenure.

Copley Family Connection

Directly tied to Michael Copley Sr and Patrick Copley through the reported 1843 200-acre agreement with Weeden Hoffman.
Near-origin point for later continuity through John Copley and descendants.
Geographic backdrop to 1900 Copley Oil Strike lease and land-history questions.

Research Resources

Lewis County Clerk deed and plat books.
Historic topo/cadastral maps for Copley Road and adjacent tracts.
Local historical society collections and WV geological records.

Acquisition Strategy

Obtain certified copy of the 1843 transaction instrument.
Georeference parcel descriptions to modern map layers.
Correlate parcel transfers with probate and oil-lease records.

Geographic Information

Current administration: County Roscommon, Connacht, Republic of Ireland.
Historical names/boundaries: Kilgefin/Kilgeffan spellings appear in older ecclesiastical records.




Historical Context (1800s-1900s)
Kilgefin sat in a rural tenant-farming economy shaped by tithe pressure, insecure tenure, and heavy emigration. In this period, Roscommon families often left through chain migration before and after the Famine.

Economy: subsistence agriculture and tenancy.
Migration pattern: high out-migration to U.S. labor corridors.
Record challenge: fragmented Catholic registers.

Copley Family Connection

Reported birthplace of Michael Copley Sr (1813).
Sibling-origin context for Patrick Copley, Bridget Copley Reynolds, Catherine Kitty Copley Hannon, William Copley.
Foundation point of the immigrant branch later established in West Virginia.

Griffith’s Valuation: www.askaboutireland.ie/griffith-valuation/
Strokestown Park / estate-paper pathways for tenant lists.

Acquisition Strategy

Run surname-variant parish sweeps (Copley/Copely/Copeley).
Correlate tithe + valuation data with sibling-age hypotheses.
Add local-archivist or professional genealogist lookups for parish gaps.

Historical names/boundaries: records can appear under Kinawley civil/parish forms with cross-border administrative complexity.

Historical Context (1800s-1900s)
Kinawley was part of a rural borderland economy with heavy dependence on small farming. Family movement from this area into transatlantic migration streams was common in the early 19th century.

Economy: agricultural and parish-centered community life.
Migration pattern: family-based emigration to U.S. river/rail corridors.
Record challenge: scattered Catholic and civil archive pathways across jurisdictions.

Copley Family Connection

Critical origin node for verifying the Munday line.

PRONI (Public Record Office of Northern Ireland): parish/civil holdings.
NLI registers portal (cross-checking Catholic records): registers.nli.ie
Local Fermanagh genealogy societies and parish transcription projects.
Newspaper databases for surname variants tied to migration narratives.

Acquisition Strategy

Build Kinawley Munday/Monday surname matrix (1820-1850).
Cross-match with Potomac-corridor census clusters in U.S. records.
Prioritize baptisms/marriages that identify parent names and witnesses.

Multigenerational continuity through John Copley and descendants.

RQ-M5-PHASE-2-FINDINGS.title: RQ-M5-PHASE-2-FINDINGS
RQ-M5-PHASE-2-FINDINGS.content:
RQ-M5 Research: Phase 2 Findings
Search Kinawley Parish, County Fermanagh
Date: April 23, 2026
Researcher: Claude Code
Question: Was Ann Munday actually Ann Murray?

Critical Documentary Findings
1. Griffith’s Valuation (1862), Kinawley Parish, County Fermanagh
Records Searched Visualization:
graph TB
    GV["Griffith's Valuation<br/>Kinawley Parish<br/>County Fermanagh<br/>(1862)"]
    
    GV --> SEARCH["Exhaustive search<br/>all surnames"]
    
    SEARCH --> MUNDAY["Search: MUNDAY<br/>or variants"]
    SEARCH --> MURRAY["Search: MURRAY<br/>or variants"]
    
    MUNDAY --> MUN_RESULT["❌ ZERO entries<br/>in Kinawley"]
    MURRAY --> MUR_RESULT["✅ 9+ entries<br/>across townlands"]
    
    MUR_RESULT --> DETAIL["Aghyoule: Bridget, Edward,<br/>Mary, Patrick, Peter<br/>Derryhooly: James<br/>Drumettagh: James<br/>Glasdrumman: John<br/>Corry: Michael"]
    
    style GV fill:#e3f2fd
    style MUN_RESULT fill:#ffcdd2
    style MUR_RESULT fill:#c8e6c9
    style DETAIL fill:#f1f8e9

MUNDAY SURNAME: ❌ NOT FOUND

Zero entries for any “Munday” or variant spelling in Kinawley
Exhaustive search of 1862 property holders by irishgenealogyhub.com

MURRAY SURNAME: ✅ 9+ DOCUMENTED ENTRIES

Bridget L: Aghyoule
Edward L: Aghyoule
James L: Derryhooly
James L: Drumettagh
John L: Glasdrumman
Mary L: Aghyoule
Michael L: Corry
Patrick L: Aghyoule (multiple entries)
Peter L: Aghyoule

Implication: The Murray family was a significant property-holding presence in Kinawley in 1862. The complete absence of “Munday” is notable.

2. Kinawley Catholic Parish Records — Critical Gap
Records Availability Timeline:
timeline
    title Kinawley Parish Records Gap
    
    1813-1835 : Ann's birth circa 1823-1824 : ❌ NO RECORDS
    1835 : Dec 11, 1835 : Catholic records BEGIN
    1835-1862 : Records 1835-1862 : ✅ Available
    1862 : Griffith's Valuation : ✅ Available
    
    Section Ann's birth circa 1823
        Baptism impossible to verify
        ~11-12 year gap with first parish records

AVAILABLE RECORDS: December 11, 1835 onwards (baptisms and marriages)

Source: PRONI MIC.1D/78; NLI Pos. 5346

ANN’S BAPTISM WINDOW: c. 1823-1824

Predates all available parish records by ~11–12 years

IMPLICATION: Ann’s baptism cannot be verified through Catholic parish records. This is a fundamental research limitation.

3. Tithe Applotment Books (1823–1837) — Next Search
STATUS: Not yet searched directly

Available at: titheapplotmentbooks.nationalarchives.ie
Searchable by surname, county (Fermanagh), parish (Kinawley)
Covers the critical 1823–1837 period
Next step: Search for both “Munday” and “Murray” entries to test co-occurrence hypothesis


Emerging Verdict






























EvidenceStatusImplication”Munday” in Griffith’s Valuation (1862)❌ Not foundNo Munday property-holding family in Kinawley by 1862”Murray” in Griffith’s Valuation (1862)✅ 9+ entriesStrong Murray family presence in KinawleyParish records for Ann’s baptism (c. 1823)❌ UnavailableCannot verify Munday via church recordsTithe Applotment Books (1823–1837)⏳ Not yet searchedCould show Munday/Murray co-occurrence before 1862
Working hypothesis (tentative): If “Munday” does not appear in the Tithe Applotment Books either, the evidence strongly suggests that “Munday” was either:

An error or transcription of “Murray”
A surname from a different townland/parish entirely
A surname from a very poor family without property holdings in tithe or valuation records


Next Actions


Search Tithe Applotment Books (1823–1837) at titheapplotmentbooks.nationalarchives.ie for:

“Munday” surname (all variants: Monday, Munde, etc.)
“Murray” surname
Test for co-occurrence in same townlands



If no Munday in Tithes: Proceed to Phase 3 — search Lewis County records and St. Michael’s Church records for evidence connecting Murray family (Fermanagh or Roscommon) to Ann


If Munday found in Tithes: Extract full details (townland, property details) and cross-reference with ship manifests and Lewis County records



Sources Accessed

Irish Genealogy Hub — Griffith’s Valuation, Kinawley Parish
Tithe Applotment Books, 1823-37 — National Archives of Ireland
FamilySearch — Kinawley Civil Parish, County Fermanagh


Assessment
Confidence Level: MODERATE
The absence of “Munday” in Griffith’s Valuation (1862) is noteworthy but not conclusive:

Munday could have emigrated before 1862 (the family left for America ~1837-1838)
Munday could have been a non-property-holding family (laborers, servants, etc.)
Munday could be a transcription of Murray that was recorded incorrectly in American family documents

The Tithe Applotment Books search is critical — it covers the period just before emigration and may reveal whether “Munday” existed independently or is a variant of “Murray.”
